alternates: Ṣalāḥ ad-Dīn Yūsuf ibn Ayyūb alarabi

wikidataDescription.en: Sultan of Egypt and founder of the Ayyubid dynasty (1137–1193)
wikidataDescription.cn: 阿尤布王朝第一位苏丹 (1137–1193)

summary.en: Kurdish-Arab military leader who united Egypt and Syria under the Ayyubid dynasty. Recaptured Jerusalem from the Crusaders in 1187 (Battle of Hattin → Siege of Jerusalem), triggering the Third Crusade. Famous in both Muslim and Christian sources for chivalric treatment of enemies.
summary.cn: 库尔德-阿拉伯裔的军事领袖，建立 Ayyubid 王朝，统一埃及与叙利亚。1187 年 Hattin 战役后重夺耶路撒冷（被十字军占领 88 年后），引发第三次十字军东征。穆斯林与基督教史料里都以善待敌人闻名。

commonMisconceptions.claim: Saladin was 'the noble Muslim' to contrast with 'barbaric Crusaders'
commonMisconceptions.correction: This is a 19th-century Romantic invention (Walter Scott's Talisman 1825). Saladin's actual reputation in his own time was mixed: he ordered massacres of certain garrisons (e.g., Templar/Hospitaller captives after Hattin), and was criticized by some Muslim contemporaries for being too lenient with Christian civilians in Jerusalem. He was a politically pragmatic commander, not a saint. ALSO: framing Crusaders as uniformly barbaric ignores complex period reality (some respected local Christians/Muslims, intermarriage occurred, Frankish nobility partly assimilated).

Saladin was ethnically Kurdish, born in Tikrit (modern Iraq). Operated within Arab-Islamic culture and patronized Arabic scholarship, but his ethnic identity was Kurdish — relevant for modern Kurdish nationalism that claims him.

neutralityNotes: Avoid 'noble Saladin vs evil Crusaders' framing — this is Western Romantic literary tradition (Walter Scott), not history. Equally avoid the reverse 'Crusader heroes vs Muslim invaders' — Crusaders were the invaders by any geographic standard. Frame both sides as complex political actors operating within their period's logic. Note: in this period the cultural/scholarly center of the Mediterranean was Islamic (Baghdad's House of Wisdom, Cordoba), not Christian Europe. But this should be stated as fact, not as 'East was superior' claim.

primarySourceQuotes.text: I shall cross this sea to their islands to pursue them until there remains no one on the face of the earth who does not acknowledge Allah, or until I die.
primarySourceQuotes.context: Reported by Saladin's biographer Baha ad-Din Ibn Shaddad. Possibly polemical exaggeration — typical of period biographies of leaders.
